nativeRange.habitat: Warm, shallow, clear to moderately turbid streams and pools over gravel and rocky substrate with aquatic vegetation; historically the Teuchitlán River system (Río Ameca basin) near the town of Tequila and Teuchitlán in Jalisco, Mexico

decorPreferences: fine gravel or rounded pebble substrate
driftwood or flat rocks as shelter
moderate water movement to simulate stream conditions

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of prepared and live foods. Offer high-quality micro-pellets or finely crushed flake as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, and micro-worms. Algae-based foods (spirulina flake, blanched spinach or zucchini) are beneficial given the species’ natural inclination to graze on algae and detritus. Variety in diet supports condition of breeding females and fry development.

goodWith: other peaceful Goodeid livebearers (Ameca splendens, Xenotoca eiseni)
avoidWith: very small nano fish that may be outcompeted for food

imageCandidates.notes: Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric, 4032x2272. Vibrant male, whole fish in frame over open gravel, sharp; clearly shows the diagnostic orange caudal fin with black subterminal band and pale terminal margin, plus orange-edged anal fin.
Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric, 4032x2272. Male with intensely coloured orange caudal fin showing the black band and clear margin; sharp on the fish, body partly among fine plants.
Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric, 4032x2272. Sharp lateral view showing the gold mid-lateral spot row and reddish cheek/flank colour; adds body-pattern detail as a second angle.

summary: The Tequila Splitfin is a critically endangered Mexican Goodeid livebearer once declared extinct in the wild. Captive breeding programs led by hobbyist networks preserved the species during its wild disappearance; successful reintroductions to the Teuchitlán River began after its 2008 rediscovery. Care is intermediate in difficulty.
careNotes:
Zoogoneticus tequila is a small Mexican highland livebearer belonging to the family Goodeidae — commonly called the splitfins — named for their distinctively forked trophotaeniae (placental-like internal structures that nourish developing fry in utero). The species is named for the Tequila Volcano region of Jalisco, near the Teuchitlán River system where it was originally collected. It was declared extinct in the wild in the late 20th century due to habitat degradation, water extraction, and competition from introduced poeciliids and tilapia. During this period, the species survived exclusively in hobbyist collections, principally through organized captive breeding efforts coordinated by the Goodeid Working Group (GWG). Wild populations were rediscovered in 2008, and coordinated reintroduction programs have since returned captive-bred fish to the Teuchitlán River. Keeping this species therefore carries genuine conservation significance.

Care is straightforward for an experienced freshwater aquarist. A tank of at least 60 L is adequate for a small group; larger is preferable for stable water quality and to accommodate the social structure of the species. Water should be clean, well-oxygenated, and moderately hard to hard (gH 10–20) at neutral to slightly alkaline pH (7.0–8.0). Temperature of 20–24°C is ideal; excessively warm water (above 26°C) shortens lifespan and can impair reproduction. The species does well with moderate flow. Dense planting provides cover for females and fry. A ratio of two or more females per male is recommended to distribute male attention. Males exhibit orange to red-orange coloration on caudal and anal fins, with a characteristic dark marginal band. Females are larger and less colorful. The species is commonly regarded as a manageable introduction to Goodeid husbandry, given its moderate adult size and the added motivation of supporting a critically endangered fish through captive care.
breedingNotes: Zoogoneticus tequila is a true Goodeid livebearer: fertilization is internal and embryos are nourished in utero via trophotaeniae — ribbon-like extensions from the embryo’s anal region that interface with the maternal ovarian tissue. Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks at 22–24°C. Broods are small by livebearer standards, typically 10–30 fry. Fry are born relatively large and well-developed, making survival straightforward; they accept micro-pellets and finely crushed flake from birth. No parental care is given and adults may eat fry — dense planting or a breeding trap significantly improves fry survival. Sexual maturity is reached at roughly 3–4 months. Breeding difficulty is moderate: obtaining consistent broods requires stable water conditions and good diet, and maintaining genetic diversity is important given the species’ critically endangered status. Hobbyists keeping this species are encouraged to connect with the Goodeid Working Group (GWG) or regional studbook coordinators to participate in managed captive breeding efforts.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Zoogoneticus tequila
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Río Ameca basin, Jalisco, Mexico), water parameters (temperature 18–26°C, pH 7.0–8.0), adult size (males to 5.5 cm, females to 7 cm), habitat description (shallow, warm, clear to turbid highland streams), diet, breeding biology including trophotaeniae, and conservation status notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Zoogoneticus tequila
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (max ~7 cm TL), native range, IUCN status (Critically Endangered), and diet notes. Confirmed viviparous reproductive mode.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Zoogoneticus tequila
additional.notes: Confirmed Critically Endangered (CR) status, documented wild extinction in late 20th century and 2008 rediscovery in Teuchitlán River, Jalisco. Notes ongoing reintroduction program and threats from habitat destruction, introduced species (Poeciliidae, tilapia), and water extraction. Highlights role of ex-situ captive populations in species survival.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Goodeid Working Group and Tequila Splitfin conservation
additional.notes: Documented the Goodeid Working Group (GWG) hobbyist conservation network’s role in maintaining captive populations of Z. tequila during the species’ wild extinction period and coordinating reintroductions. Confirmed captive breeding difficulty rating (moderate), brood size (10–30 fry), and husbandry recommendations for Goodeid livebearers.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Livebearer Care and Breeding
additional.notes: General livebearer husbandry guide used to cross-check diet, tank size recommendations, and female-to-male ratio guidance applicable to Goodeid livebearers. No dedicated Z. tequila page exists; data supplementary only.

fish.breedingNotes: Zoogoneticus tequila reproduces via true Goodeid viviparity, nourishing embryos internally through trophotaeniae. Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks at typical temperatures, with broods of 10–30 fry. Fry are born well-developed and accept finely crushed foods from birth. Consistent reproduction requires stable water conditions and a varied diet. Given the species' critically endangered status, hobbyists are encouraged to manage genetic diversity carefully and connect with the Goodeid Working Group studbook.
